data.content: Need to add `ImplicitUsings`. Let me fix the project file and re-run:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Fix project and re-run stackalloc test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Fix project and re-run stackalloc test

arguments.description: Fix project and re-run stackalloc test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args:
{"command": "cat << 'EOF' > /tmp/stackalloc_nint_test/stackalloc_nint_test.csproj
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <AllowUnsafeBlocks>true</AllowUnsafeBlocks>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
EOF
dotnet run --project /tmp/stackalloc_nint_test", "description": "Fix project and re-run stackalloc test", "initial_wait": 30}

toolArgs.description: Fix project and re-run stackalloc test

data.content:
**Yes, C# fully supports `stackalloc`'d `Span` of native-sized integers.** Both `nint` and `nuint` work:

```csharp
Span<nint>  a = stackalloc nint[4];   // ✅ works
Span<nuint> b = stackalloc nuint[4];  // ✅ works
```

The test confirmed correct values including `nint.MaxValue` (2⁶³−1) and `nuint.MaxValue` (2⁶⁴−1) on this 64-bit platform. This has been supported since C# 9 / .NET 5, when `nint`/`nuint` were introduced as first-class unmanaged types (which is the constraint `stackalloc` requires).
